summary: Spolsky argues against what he calls 'Econ 101 management' — the practice of using financial incentives and punishments to motivate workers. He explains three major problems: extrinsic rewards displace stronger intrinsic motivation through the Overjustification Effect, workers game metrics to optimize for measured targets rather than actual goals, and incentive schemes represent an abdication of real management responsibility. Drawing on Robert Austin's research, he contends that no amount of metric tweaking can fix these problems. Instead, managers should build systems and training that help people do better work, rather than bribing them to figure it out on their own.
key_points: Financial incentives displace intrinsic motivation through the Overjustification Effect, actually reducing workers' desire to do good work
Workers inevitably game any metric system, optimizing for what's measured rather than what management actually wants
Robert Austin's research shows metric-based incentives always backfire — first you get what you want, then you get something worse
Incentive-based management is an abdication of responsibility — managers should build systems and train people rather than bribing them
Starbucks' success comes from HQ designing a better system for workers, not from paying bonuses for fewer order mistakes
money_quotes: The Econ 101 manager assumes that everyone is motivated by money, and that the best way to get people to do what you want them to do is to give them financial rewards and punishments to create incentives.
When you offer people money to do things that they wanted to do, anyway, they suffer from something called the Overjustification Effect.
Since extrinsic motivation is a much weaker effect, the net result is that you've actually reduced their desire to do a good job.
Developers are clever this way. Whatever you try to measure, they'll find a way to maximize, and you'll never quite get what you want.
The biggest problem with Econ 101 management, though, is that it's not management at all: it's really more of an abdication of management.
Instead of training developers on techniques of writing reliable code, you just absolve yourself of responsibility by paying them if they do.
As a manager it's your job to figure out a system. That's Why You Get The Big Bucks.
Start doing your job managing and stop feeding your chickens kopecks.
key_insight: Using financial incentives to motivate workers is not management — it's an abdication of the manager's real job, which is to build systems and training that enable people to do better work.
